coredata.dc:title: Strong enhancement of the chemiluminescence of cerium (IV)-Na2S system by mono-dispersed N-CDs generated in situ
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Xiaohan
Yan, Shuguang
Wei, Chudong
Zhang, Yanyan
Su, Yingying
Lv, Yi
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was observed to strongly amplify the chemiluminescence (CL) intensity of Ce (IV)-Na2S system with an enhancement factor around 55. Possible mechanism was explored and discussed on account of results of free radical scavenger experiments, CL spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), etc. During the CL process, mono-dispersed N-CDs generated in situ catalyzed the production of more 1O2 on its surface and the CL enhancement mainly attributed to the chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) between (1O2)2*/Ce (III)* and N-CDs. The new CL system was then developed as a convenient and sensitive platform for H2S detection. The linear range was 20–200 nM and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.32 nM. The applications in different water samples were proved to have good recovery rate (tap water: 100.46%-109.22%; pool water: 99.91%-102.87%) with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Cerium (IV)
H2S
Mono-dispersed N-CDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of the CL experimental conditions
Analytical performance and application in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21874094 & 22074098) are gratefully acknowledged. We thank Dr. Chen Chen, Dr. Xiaobin Xie and Dr. Yanju Luo in Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for technical assistance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra and (B) UV–vis spectrum of N-CDs.
(A) XPS spectra for N-CDs. (B) The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s, (C) N1s and (D) O 1 s of N-CDs.

(A) The CL intensity of (a) N-CDs-Na2S system, (b) Ce (IV)-N-CDs system, (c) Ce (IV)-Na2S system and (d) Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs system. (B)The CL emission of different injection orders of Ce (IV), Na2S and N-CDs. (a) Injecting N-CDs solution to the mixture of Ce (IV) and Na2S; (b) Injecting Na2S solution to the mixture of Ce (IV) and N-CDs; (c) Injecting Ce (IV) solution to the mixture of Na2S and N-CDs. (C) The CL kinetic curves of (a) Ce (IV)-Na2S-NMP system and (b) Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs system. Ce4+: 8 mM, Na2S: 1 μM, N-CDs and NMP: 10 μg/mL. (D) CL spectra of Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs system.

(A) Effects of some coexistent metal ions on the detection of H2S, (B) CL intensities of Ce (IV)-Na2S system toward different anions, (C) The CL intensities of Ce (IV)-Na2S system on various concentrations of Na2S (20–200 nM) and (D) the linear curve between CL intensity and Na2S. Ce4+: 8 mM, Na2S: 1 μM, N-CDs: 10 μg/mL, all metal ions and anions: 100 μM.
Schematic illustration of the CL mechanisms of Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs system.
Results for the determination of H2S in real samples.

head.ce:title: Strong enhancement of the chemiluminescence of cerium (IV)-Na2S system by mono-dispersed N-CDs generated in situ

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical & Testing Center, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was observed to strongly amplify the chemiluminescence (CL) intensity of Ce (IV)-Na2S system with an enhancement factor around 55. Possible mechanism was explored and discussed on account of results of free radical scavenger experiments, CL spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), etc. During the CL process, mono-dispersed N-CDs generated in situ catalyzed the production of more 1O2 on its surface and the CL enhancement mainly attributed to the chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CRET) between (1O2)2*/Ce (III)* and N-CDs. The new CL system was then developed as a convenient and sensitive platform for H2S detection. The linear range was 20–200 nM and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.32 nM. The applications in different water samples were proved to have good recovery rate (tap water: 100.46%-109.22%; pool water: 99.91%-102.87%) with satisfactory results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CL, the light emission induced by chemical reactions, has superiorities of high sensitivity, low background signal, short response time, easy operation and low cost of instrumentation [1]. Ce (IV)-involved CL system is one of the most classical CL systems. Ce (IV) could react with common reductive sulfur containing substances such as sodium sulfite (Na2SO3), sodium hydrosulfite (NaHSO3) or sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3) to produce CL. In these systems, Na2SO3/NaHSO3/Na2S2O3 was oxidized to the excited state SO2 (SO2*) and the relaxation of luminescent intermediate SO2* to the ground state contributed to CL emission [2–4]. Unfortunately, low luminescence efficiency of SO2* results in weak CL intensity which seriously limits further development and intense researches have been made to expand the range of their applications. Up to now, the most frequently used technique is constructing CRET systems via energy transfer from the redox reaction by using fluorescent molecules or Tb3+-fluoroquinolone complexes and luminescent nanomaterials as energy acceptors, which has been developed as an effective method in analyzing fluorescent organic compounds such as ciprofloxacin and benzamides, sulfite in PM2.5 and ions, etc. [3–7]. The exploration of new CL system is necessary to further expand the application of CL, and is always one of the hot issues in CL field. Na2S is also one of the common sulfur containing substances with strong reducibility and it should produce CL when reacts with Ce (IV). However, to the best of our knowledge, the Ce (IV)-Na2S CL system hasn’t been investigated yet.

Fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials have been intensively researched thanks to their fascinating properties such as stable photoluminescence, high chemical stability, low cytotoxicity and great biocompatibility. And carbon dots (CDs) have become a research hotspot of carbon-based nanomaterials because of its interesting physicochemical properties and outstanding luminescent performance with multifarious applications in bioimaging, sensing, catalyzing, drugs delivering, etc. [8–11]. In recent years, CDs have been an attractive topic and a hot spot in the field of CL benefiting from good aqueous solubility, tunable emission wavelength and high photostability which take part in several CL reactions with different roles such as emitting species, energy acceptors or catalysts [12–15]. Lin’s group first investigated the remarkable CL property of CDs in the presence of peroxynitrous acid (ONOOH) since 2011 and proposed a promising CL method for nitrite determination [16]. They later discovered CDs could exhibit attractive CL behaviors in the presence of acidic potassium permanganate (KMnO4) and cerium (IV) or even in a strong alkaline condition [17,18]. Since then, the development of CDs-based CL has been greatly boosted and its applications in analyzing, sensing and bioimaging or even in information encrypting have been brought into focus increasingly [14,19–21]. However, low quantum yield (QY), short and monochromatic emission have limited its further applications. These drawbacks have been improved through surface modification and element doping [22,23]. And element doping (especially nitrogen doping by using nitrogen containing precursors) as an effective method is frequently adopted to provide CDs with improved quantum efficiency and tunable fluorescence emission band, the introduction of N endows the materials with high QY and high catalytic activity [24,25]. And N-CDs now also performed well in CL fields, which has developed rapidly [26,27].

In this work, N-CDs was synthesized by NMP through a facile hydrothermal method. A weak CL emission between Ce (IV) and Na2S was observed for the first time and the mono-dispersed N-CDs generated in situ was found to have significant enhancement on the Ce (IV)-Na2S CL system. Thus, a new CL system was fabricated and the possible mechanism was detailly discussed. The preliminary investigations evidenced that the CL enhancement was mainly ascribed to the CRET between (1O2)2*/Ce (III)* and N-CDs. This CL system has great potential in the sensitive and convenient determination of H2S in tap water and pool water. Additionally, chemical energy produced from CL reaction was found to contribute to uniform dispersion of N-CDs, which provides a novel perspective on study of CL mechanism involving nanomaterials.

N-Methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) weas purchased from Chengdu Chemical Regent Co. Ltd. Ceric ammonium nitrate (Ce(NH4)2(NO3)6), ascorbic acid (AA), sodium azide (NaN3) were obtained from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) and nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) and were bought from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). The stock solution of sodium sulfide (Na2S) (11.5 mg/L) was purchased from Tanmo Quality Inspection Reference Material Center (TMRM). Other reagents were supplied by Chengdu United Institute of Chemical & Reagent. Ultrapure water (conductivity: 18.2 MΩ/cm) was homemade from the Milli-Q water purification system (ULUPURE, Chengdu, China). All obtained regents were at least of analytical grade without extra purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken by JEM-2010 microscope (JEOL Co., Japan), 200 kV. The element analysis was characterized by XPS recorded by an ESCA Lab 250Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA), using monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1361 eV). The functional groups of samples were analyzed by IS10 Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Bruker Co., Germany). All Fluorescence spectra and CL spectra were obtained by F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Japan) and the UV–Vis absorbance was measured by U-2910 UV − vis spectrometer (Hitachi Co., Japan).

N-CDs was prepared in a reported hydrothermal method with some modification [28]. NMP was used as the source material and N-CDs was obtained without extra addition of oxidant or strong acid, base or oxidant. Firstly, 30 mL NMP was directly added into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with the heating in an oven (180 ℃, 10 h). Then the as-prepared dispersion showed yellowish-brown color cooled down naturally to ambient temperature and was dialyzed 12 h with a dialysis membrane (MWCO:500 Da) in ultrapure water to get purified N-CDs solution. Ultrapure water was replaced for 4 times Finally, rotary evaporation was performed on the purified N-CDs solution by rotary evaporator to collect solid N-CDs. The final product was stored in a test tube for further use.

The luminescence analyzer (BPCL-2-TGC, Beijing, China) was used to conduct all the CL measurements. In a typical CL experiment, 100 μL Na2S and 100 μL N-CDs were premixed in a 2 mL quartz cuvette, then 100 μL Ce(NH4)2(NO3)6 mixed with 700 μL ultrapure water were quickly injected by a flow pump (BT100-02, Baoding, China). Photomultiplier tube (PMT, Hamamatsu, Japan) was taken to record the CL signals and the data was collected then converted to the computer for acquisition. The PMT voltage was set as 1250 V with an interval of 0.1 s for CL intensity collection.

Tap water was taken from our laboratory in Analytical ＆Testing Center, Sichuan University. And the pool water was collected from the pond in front of the Yifu Building located in Sichuan University. Both tap water and pool water were filtered by 0.22 μm membranes to remove large particles in the samples. And no other treatment was performed on the water samples before analysis. After calculation, various volumes of Na2S stock solution were added into 10 mL water samples to prepare three different concentration spiked samples (40 nM, 100 nM and 150 nM) and were directly used for later detection.

UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectra were first used to explore the optical characteristics of N-CDs in aqueous solution. Detailed photoluminescence (PL) spectra was shown in Fig. 1 A and typical fluorescent spectra of quantum dots was exhibited due to the surface energy traps or quantum effect of the materials [29,30]. Excitation-dependent emission of N-CDs was observed with the excitation from 300 to 410 nm as previously reported. And the max emission at 460 nm was monitored when N-CDs was excited by 360 nm [16]. In respect of the UV spectrum (Fig. 1B), N-CDs displayed two broad UV–vis absorption bands around 280 nm and 340 nm, the former corresponded to π-π* transition of C = C conjugated band and the latter belonged to n-π* transition of C = O band [31,32]. Additionally, N-CDs showed bright and blue emission under 365 nm ultraviolet radiation in the inset of Fig. 1B.

In addition, the morphology, size distribution and crystallography of as-obtained samples were analyzed by HRTEM. The HRTEM image (Fig. S1A) reveals that the materials were in spherical but not so well mono-dispersed with an average size around 3 nm. Besides, distinct lattice fringes could be observed and the interplanar distance was about 0.32 nm (the inset from Fig. S1A), which was in accordance with lattice spacing of N-CDs in the reported literature, belonging to the (002) face of graphite [33].

Moreover, FTIR spectra recorded from 500 to 4000 cm−1 was illustrated in Fig. S2 to figure out surface groups in the materials. The wide absorption band between 3500 and 3000 cm−1 corresponded to O–H and N–H stretches. The obvious peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 2854 cm−1 were both attributed to C–H bond stretching vibrations. And C=O stretching vibration in -COOH was exhibited at 1648 cm-1and the symmetric carboxylate stretch was displayed at 1380 cm−1 [34,35]. And the peak at 1436 cm−1, 1300 cm−1 and 1030 cm−1 could be assigned to bending vibration of C-N, C-H and stretching vibration of C-O-C, respectively. [27]. These results suggested that abundant reactive groups (–OH, –COOH, and –NH2) existed on the surface of the as-synthesized materials, endowing good solubility, desirable stability and active sites to the N-CDs.

What’s more, the resulting materials’ elemental composition and surface functional states were also studied by XPS in detail. As depicted in Fig. 2 A, the whole XPS spectrum demonstrated that C, N and O were the main elements of materials, verifying the successful incorporation of N. And the binding energy located at 284.8, 399.9 and 531.9 eV were respectively corresponded to C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s. Four peaks located at the binding energy of 284.1, 285.1, 286.6 and 288.2 eV in C 1 s spectra belonged to C-C/C=C, C-N, C-O and C=O, respectively (Fig. 2B). And N 1 s spectra via peak fitting were decomposed into N-C centered at 399.4 eV and N-H at 401.2 eV (Fig. 2C). Additionally, as for the O 1 s spectra in Fig. 2D, the convolution peaks displayed in 531.2 and 532.8 eV were respectively to C=O and C-O [27,36]. The characterization results of XPS were proved to be consistent with those of FTIR, further indicated the as-made samples were rich in –OH, –COOH, –NH2. Therefore, all these characteristics and analysis above validated the successful synthesis of N-CDs.

The CL phenomenon is illustrated in Fig. 3 A. No CL signal except for the baseline was observed between Na2S and N-CDs which revealed that there was no reaction between the two solutions (a). Only weak CL signals were obtained when Ce (IV) was injected into N-CDs (b) or Na2S (c). The highest CL emission was detected when injecting Ce (IV) into the mixture of Na2S and N-CDs (d). Compared with Ce (IV)-Na2S system, almost 55 times enhancement on the CL intensity was obtained, suggesting that N-CDs was of great significance in amplifying CL emission. And other injection orders were also discussed as shown in Fig. 3B, no matter adding N-CDs to the mixture of Ce (IV) and Na2S (a) or injecting Na2S into the mixture Ce (IV) and N-CDs (b), only weaker CL intensities were displayed because Ce (IV) had reacted with Na2S or N-CDs before the addition of the third solution. In addition, a control experiment was also investigated, as could been seen from Fig. 3C, only slight enhancement was monitored in the Ce (IV)-Na2S-NMP CL system when N-CDs was replaced by equivalent pure NMP. These data revealed that the participation of N-CDs contributed to the enhancement of CL intensity rather than the carbon sources.

Free radical scavenger experiments were first carried out to figure out the mechanism of this phenomenon. As shown in Fig. S3A, when 2 mM AA (scavenger of all free radicals) was employed, the CL intensity decreased almost 100%, confirming that free radicals were indispensable in the CL emission. Strong inhibition toward CL intensity emerged when NaN3 (a well-known radical scavenger for 1O2) was added, which indicated that 1O2 was the main reactive oxygen radicals in the CL system. Among the quenchers, tert-Butanol (TBA) and ethanol (EtOH) are frequently used as sensitive radicals to distinguish •OH and SO4 •-, the former mainly quenches •OH while the latter scavenges both •OH and SO4 •- [37]. The presence of TBA weakened the CL intensity illustrated that •OH contributed to the CL process. But when the same concentration of EtOH was injected, no obvious reduce showed of the CL signal compared with TBA, revealing that the contribution of SO4 •- could be neglected. When only 0.2 mM NBT (appropriative trapping agent for O2 •−) was employed, the CL signal was suppressed completely, indicating that O2 •− might participate in the CL process (Fig. S3B). It was reported that if O2 •− was trapped by NBT, a characteristic absorption band at 550 nm would appear in UV–vis [38]. However, as Fig. S4A exhibited, no absorption at 550 nm appeared which suggested that O2 •− was not responsible for the CL system. And Fig. S4B showed that apparent yellow precipitate formed when NBT was added into Ce4+ solution, so it was speculated that the obvious quenching effect could be ascribed to the chelation between NBT and Ce4+. Accordingly, it was certain that 1O2 and •OH as critical intermediates took part in the Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs CL system.

Besides, dissolved oxygen (DO) experiment was explored to analysis whether dissolved oxygen was associated with the CL reaction. The reaction solution for comparison were bubbled with N2 or O2 at least 30 min before CL measurements (The solution of Na2S was only bubbled with N2 because it could be oxidized by O2). According to the results displayed in Fig. S5, compared with untreated solutions, the CL intensity of solutions bubbles with O2 showed slight increase, while that of solutions treated with N2 decreased obviously (about 20%). Thus, DO was indispensable in the CL reaction.

What’s more, N-CDs’ function in the Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs CL system was further exploited by employing UV–vis to detect the 1O2 concentration changes. Thus, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), an effective trapping reagent for active 1O2 with a characteristic absorption at 420 nm was applied. The absorption intensity of DPBF would be diminished if 1O2 was trapped through a Diels − Alder 1,4-cycloaddition reaction [39]. As Fig. S6 displayed, the distinctive absorption peak slightly decreased in Ce (IV)-Na2S CL system but significantly weakened when N-CDs was added, and with the concentration of N-CDs increased the absorption gradually decreased. These facts not only effectively manifested that 1O2 was one of the donors of the CL emission but also proved that N-CDs facilitated the production of 1O2.

Then the CL spectra was measured by F-7000 to further identify the emission species of the Ce (IV)-Na2S and Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs CL systems. The CL spectra of the former system was too weak to be captured by the instrument. In view of Fig. 4 D, the CL spectrum of Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs CL system was centered around at 460 nm, which was coincident with the fluorescence of N-CDs (λex = 360 nm, λem = 460 nm). The light emission of dimol singlet-oxygen (1O2)2* was located at 381, 477 and 578 nm, which was not match well with the CL spectra although the presence of 1O2 was proved by scavenger experiment [40]. We suspected that CRET between (1O2)2* and N-CDs occurred because the 381 nm emission wavelength of (1O2)2* could excite N-CDs. Therefore, the main CL emitter in the system was reasonably inferred as the excited-state N-CDs (N-CDs*).

And the N-CDs after reaction was also explored by TEM and XPS to better explore the mechanism. From the HRTEM image in Fig. S1B we can see that the average size and d-space of the materials remain unchanged but become more uniformly dispersed, which allows more 1O2 to be produced on its surface, thus producing stronger CL. According to C 1 s XPS pattern in Fig. S7A, the peak attributed to C=O increased, manifesting part of the surface hydroxyl groups were oxidized and changed into carbonyl groups. And the decrease of C-N might be explained by the break of C-N bond during the oxidization by Ce (IV) of N-CDs.

Based on these discussions, possible mechanisms of the Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs system were put forward and summarized in Scheme 1 . The CL of the system arose from three main routes, the CRET between (1O2)2* and the N-CDs, the CRET between Ce (III)* and the N-CDs, and the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and electrons in N-CDs.

Firstly, Ce (IV) reacted with HS- and dissolved oxygen to produce excited Ce (III) (Ce (III)*) and •OH (reactions (1) and (2)). And the energy was transferred from Ce (III)* produced in reaction (2) with an emission wavelength at 360 nm to N-CDs to form N-CDs*, resulting light emission (reactions (3) and (8)) [41,42]. Then Ce (IV) in solution or dissolved oxygen further oxidized HSO3 - to SO4 2- (reaction (4)). Na2S in the CL system finally changed into SO4 2- and the result was verified by high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S7B).
(1) S2- + H+ → HS-
(2) 4Ce (IV) + HS- + O2 + 3H2O → 4Ce (III)* + HSO3 - + 4H+ + 2•OH
(3) Ce (III)* + N-CDs → N-CDs* + Ce (III)
(4) Ce (IV) + HSO3 - + H2O → Ce (III) + SO4 2- + 2H+

Meanwhile, the recombination of •OH generated in reaction (2) led to 1O2 production in Ce (IV)-Na2S system (reaction (5)). And the chemical energy produced in the reaction between Ce (IV) and Na2S could make the N-CDs dispersed more uniformly. Dispersed N-CDs catalyzed more production of 1O2 on its surface which could also explain why the straight analysis of 1O2 by EPR was not successful during our experiment [43]. Then N-CDs* formed through the energy transfer from (1O2)2* and when it came back to the ground state an efficient CL emission occurred (reactions (6)-(8)). Additionally, consumption of •OH promoted the generation of Ce (III)*, thus, the energy transfer process between Ce (III)* and N-CDs could be more efficiently completed (reaction (2)).
(5) •OH + •OH → 1O2 + H2O
(6)
1O2 + 1O2 → (1O2)2*
(7) (1O2)2* + N-CDs → N-CDs* +2O2
(8) N-CDs* → N-CDs + hv (Em = 460 nm)

We inferred that there might be very small amount of sulfite radicals (•HSO3) existed in the Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs system thus SO2* (300–450 nm) also could transferred its energy to N-CDs, resulting an enhanced CL emission as previously reported [44]. But the contribution was negligible because EPR technique was used for the detection of •HSO3 with DMPO as a spin trap agent and showed no signal.

In addition, during the CL reaction, the classical oxidant Ce (IV) and •OH could also serve as hole injectors and injected holes into N-CDs which were oxidized and converted to N-CDs•+ [16,17]. Then light emission resulted through electron–hole annihilation between the thermally excited electrons in the high energy band and injected holes [32,45]. The amount of holes in N-CDs increased through hole injection and the electron–hole annihilation was also increased, contributing to the CL at 460 nm.

Thus, the enhancement of the Ce (IV)-Na2S CL system could be due to the energy transfer from Ce (III)*/ (1O2)2* to N-CDs, and the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and electrons in N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the CL experimental conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Na2S is commonly used as hydrogen sulfide donor [46], the Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs CL system exhibited excellent performance for the analysis for Na2S so various CL experimental conditions were optimized to establish a platform for H2S assay. As Fig. S8A shown, different hydrothermal treatment time (5, 10, 15 and 20 h) on NMP was first discussed, N-CDs prepared at 10 h were found to have the maximum CL emission. Then the concentration of different reaction regents was explored. The effect of Ce (IV) concentration ranged from 2 to 14 mM was examined in the CL reaction and 8 mM provided the best CL response (Fig. S8B). And for the N-CDs concentration, the highest CL signal was monitored when N-CDs was 10 µg/mL in the range from 0 to 60 µg/mL (Fig. S8C). The flow rate was also a critical parameter related to the CL intensity. As depicted in Fig. S8D, the speed of peristaltic pump from 20 to 100 rpm was tested and 100 rpm was selected because it was the highest speed of the flow pump and provided the best CL performance. In summary, the optimum quantities for the Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs CL system were 8 mM for Ce4+, 10 µg/mL for N-CDs and 100 rpm for peristaltic pump speed.

The interference of the possible coexisting species in real water samples was an essential factor that must be taken into consideration before its practical application. As depicted in Fig. 4A, 15 common coexisting metal ions (K+, Cu2+, Zr4+, Fe2+, Na2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Sn2+) only resulted in acceptable fluctuation on the CL intensity under the optimal conditions, effectively demonstrating that Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs CL system had a good anti-interference ability for H2S detection. Additionally, the selectivity of this CL system toward Na2S was evaluated as well. 11 anions (AC-, F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, C2O4 2-, PO4 3-, CO3 2–, NO2 –, NO3 –, ClO-) were chosen as potential interfering substances to investigate the selectivity of H2S analysis. Only weak CL signal occurred when these interferential species were measured while Na2S exhibited strong CL signal under the same conditions, hence high selectivity was proved as shown in Fig. 4B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance and application in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In our experiments, Na2S stock solution was diluted properly to acquire a series of H2S standard solutions within a concentration range of 0 nM to 200 nM. CL experiment for analyzing H2S was operated when all the experimental parameters were set as the favorable conditions and all the data collected and calculated were displayed as follows. As depicted in Fig. 4C, the CL signal was found to be gradually increased with the increasing addition of H2S. And as Fig. 4D displayed, an excellent liner relationship of the CL intensity enhancement (ΔICL) versus the H2S concentration ranging from 20 to 200 nM was also obtained. The corresponding linear regression equation was y = 131.78x-1842.93 (R2 = 0.998), the limit of detection (LOD) of H2S was 0.32 nM (S/N = 3), and the relative standard deviation (RSD) for of 150 nM H2S in repetitive measurements was 4.21% (n = 5). Thus, our method was in good linearity, high sensitivity and acceptable precision. When compared with other CL detective strategies, our method is sensitive enough [47–49] Therefore, we proposed that Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs CL system was of great potential in H2S determination.

A standard addition method in real samples (tap water and pool water) was further carried out to test the feasibility and reliability of the fabricated H2S analysis method. As shown in Table 1 , real water samples with three different spiked concentrations of Na2S (40 nM, 100 nM and 150 nM) were tasted. The recovery rate of H2S detection varied from 100.46% to 109.22% (RSD, 0.40%-1.64%, n = 3) for tap water samples and 99.91%-102.87% (RSD, 2.20%-3.48%, n = 3) for pool water samples respectively, which strongly demonstrated that Ce (IV)-Na2S-N-CDs CL system was a promising technique in the real sample detection of H2S.

In summary, mono-dispersed N-CDs generated in situ was found to have strong enhancing effect on Ce (IV)-Na2S CL system, which was attributed to the CRET between (1O2)2* and the N-CDs, the CRET between Ce (III)* and the N-CDs, and the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and electrons in N-CDs. This new CL system was sensitive and simple enough to be established as a H2S detection platform. And the analysis of H2S concentration in real sample waters was obtained with satisfactory results.

Xiaohan Yuan: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shuguang Yan: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Chudong Wei: Writing – review & editing. Yanyan Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Yingying Su: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yi Lv: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21874094 & 22074098) are gratefully acknowledged. We thank Dr. Chen Chen, Dr. Xiaobin Xie and Dr. Yanju Luo in Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for technical assistance.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108560.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
